era_context: The Roman Empire under Hadrian continues its consolidation and cultural patronage. The Bar Kokhba Revolt in Judaea is in its final phase. The Eastern Han dynasty in China is in the early stages of institutional decline. The Kushan Empire is at its height under Kanishka or his successors, facilitating Silk Road trade and the spread of Buddhism.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia (limited written sources for this specific year)

events.title: Bar Kokhba Revolt: final phase and siege of Betar
events.description: The Bar Kokhba Revolt enters its final phase in 134 CE. Roman forces under Sextus Julius Severus intensify their campaign, besieging the rebel stronghold at Betar (Bethar), located southwest of Jerusalem. The siege is prolonged and brutal. Archaeological and numismatic evidence suggests the final fall of Betar occurred in late 134 or early 135 CE. The revolt's suppression marks a turning point in Jewish-Roman relations and the political status of Judaea.

sources.name: Bar Kokhba letters and documents from Judean Desert caves
Numismatic evidence (coins minted by Bar Kokhba rebels)

events.certainty_note: The siege of Betar is attested in multiple sources, but the exact year of its fall is debated between 134 and 135 CE. Numismatic evidence suggests 134 CE may be more accurate, but scholarly consensus varies.
cross_references: Part of the Bar Kokhba Revolt (132–135 CE)

events.title: Hadrian's architectural patronage: Pantheon and other projects in Rome
events.description: Hadrian, known for his extensive architectural patronage, continues to oversee major building projects throughout the empire. The Pantheon in Rome, rebuilt under Hadrian (c. 126–128 CE), is now complete and stands as a masterpiece of Roman engineering and design. By 134 CE, Hadrian's Villa at Tivoli is also substantially complete, featuring innovative architectural designs and extensive gardens. These projects reflect Hadrian's cultural ambitions and his desire to leave a lasting architectural legacy.
key_figures: Apollodorus of Damascus (architect, though his role in the Pantheon is debated)

sources.name: Scriptores Historiae Augustae (Historia Augusta), Life of Hadrian
Pliny the Younger, Natural History (earlier, but references architectural practices)
Archaeological evidence from the Pantheon and Hadrian's Villa

events.certainty_note: The existence and dating of these architectural projects are well-established archaeologically. The Pantheon's completion date is traditionally placed c. 128 CE, and Hadrian's Villa construction spanned the 120s–130s CE.
cross_references: Part of Hadrian's broader cultural and architectural program (r. 117–138 CE)
Reflects Roman imperial power and cultural achievement

events.title: Kushan Empire: continued dominance of Central Asian trade routes
events.description: The Kushan Empire, under the rule of Kanishka I (r. c. 127–150 CE) or his successors, continues to dominate the Central Asian trade routes and facilitate commerce between the Roman Empire, China, and India. The Kushans mint coins with Greek, Persian, and Indian inscriptions, reflecting their multicultural character. Buddhist monasteries and stupas are patronized, and the empire serves as a crucial conduit for the transmission of Buddhism along the Silk Road. By 134 CE, Kushan power is at or near its zenith.
key_figures: Kanishka I (Kushan emperor, r. c. 127–150 CE)
Buddhist monks and scholars patronized by the Kushan court

sources.name: Chinese historical records (Hou Hanshu, compiled later)
Archaeological evidence from Kushan sites (Bactria, Gandhara)

events.certainty_note: The Kushan Empire's dominance of Central Asian trade in the 2nd century CE is well-established. However, the exact dating of Kanishka's reign is debated (some scholars place it earlier, c. 100–127 CE). The year 134 CE is assigned by convention as a marker within this period of Kushan prominence.
cross_references: Contemporary with Roman-Parthian tensions over trade routes
Facilitates the spread of Buddhism to China and beyond
Part of the broader Silk Road trade network

disconfirming_evidence: The exact year of Betar's fall is disputed; some scholars argue for 135 CE rather than 134 CE. The dating of Kanishka's reign is one of the most contentious issues in Indian history, with scholars proposing dates ranging from c. 78 CE to c. 150 CE. The traditional placement of Kanishka in the 2nd century CE is now questioned by some scholars who argue for an earlier date. This uncertainty affects the precise chronology of Kushan-Roman and Kushan-Chinese interactions.
historiographic_note: The year 134 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire and the Bar Kokhba Revolt, with multiple sources available. However, the record is heavily weighted toward military and political events. For the Kushan Empire, numismatic and archaeological evidence is rich, but written sources are limited and often come from external observers (Chinese, Roman, or later Buddhist texts). The experiences of ordinary people, women, and non-elite populations are largely absent from the historical record. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas have no synchronized written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: The siege of Betar in 134 CE leads to the final suppression of the Bar Kokhba Revolt in 135 CE
Continuation of Hadrian's architectural patronage program
Kushan dominance of Central Asian trade facilitates the spread of Buddhism eastward
